title: St. Agabus, St. Zechariah the Priest, St. Gelasius & The Martyrs of Sinai and Raithu
narrative:
title": "St. Agabus, St. Zechariah the Priest, St. Gelasius & The Martyrs of Sinai and Raithu

narrative": "title\": \"St. Agabus, St. Zechariah the Priest, St. Gelasius & The Martyrs of Sinai and Raithu

narrative\": \"St. Agabus the Prophet, St. Zechariah the Priest, St. Gelasius the Martyr, and the Martyrs of Sinai and Raithu

On this day the holy Church meditates upon the long and costly chain of prophetic witness that links the Old Covenant to the New and the New to the age of martyrdom — a chain forged by four companies of witnesses who each paid in full for the word they carried. The Prophet Agabus of the seventy disciples stands first, that man of the Spirit who bound his own hands and feet with Paul's girdle in Caesarea and declared: Thus says the Holy Spirit, so shall the Jews at Jerusalem bind the man who owns this belt. His prophetic gesture — embodied, enacted, costly in its social drama — belongs to the tradition of the great Hebrew prophets who enacted their oracles in the body, and it places Agabus at the hinge between the prophetic and apostolic ages. He did not merely speak the word; he put it on his flesh, as every true prophet must.

Zechariah the Priest, the father of the Baptist, is the second prophetic voice of this day — a man who did not so much speak prophecy as become its vessel against his will. Struck dumb by the angel Gabriel for his unbelief at the announcement of John's coming, he endured nine months of silence while Elizabeth carried the child, and when he opened his mouth at the circumcision to write the name John, his tongue was loosed and he prophesied the great canticle that the Church has sung at morning prayer ever since: Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for He has visited and redeemed His people. The Ethiopian tradition reads this silence as a purification — the dumbness of Zechariah was the stripping away of the old prophetic tongue so that a new word, wholly given by God, could emerge from it.

Saint Gelasius the Martyr is among the lesser-known witnesses whom this day commends to the faithful's discovery. The tradition preserves his story as one of those sudden, grace-wrought conversions that confound every human calculation: Gelasius was present at a theatrical performance during which a baptism was being mocked as part of the spectacle, and he, cast in the role of the one being baptised, felt at the moment of the mime such a genuine movement of grace that he stood and declared himself a Christian before the whole assembly. The crowd at first laughed, thinking it part of the act; when they realised he was in earnest, they dragged him from the stage to execution. He was baptised, as the tradition says, in his own blood.

The Martyrs of Sinai and Raithu are a company rather than individuals — monks of the holy desert of Sinai and the seaport of Raithu who were massacred by Blemmyes and Saracen raiders who swept through the peninsula, killing all they found in the hermit cells and the common monastery. The Ethiopian Synaxarium honours them alongside the forty-nine martyrs of Scetis as the great anonymous confessors of the desert, men who had gone to the ends of the earth to seek God in silence and who received instead the red martyrdom they had never sought. Their memorial in the Synaxarium is brief and their names forgotten, but the tradition insists on their commemoration because the Church's memory is one of the forms of resurrection — to remember the forgotten martyrs is to raise them, in some sense, from the oblivion to which the world consigned them. Agabus who spoke, Zechariah who was silenced, Gelasius who laughed and then died for the truth, the unnamed monks who died in the sand: together they show that the prophetic witness takes as many forms as there are human lives to bear it. Amen.
